original_abstract: Robust multi-object tracking (MOT) in video sequences fundamentally depends on the ability to model complex spatial-temporal interactions between entities. In this work, we introduce TransMOT, a novel tracking framework that utilizes graph transformers to efficiently capture these relationships. By representing object trajectories as a series of sparse weighted graphs, TransMOT facilitates the effective modeling of interactions even across dense scenes. The architecture employs a specialized spatial graph transformer encoder, a temporal transformer encoder, and a spatial graph transformer decoder to process these representations. Our approach is not only more computationally efficient than traditional Transformer architectures but also yields superior tracking precision. To further optimize both speed and robustness, we introduce a cascade association mechanism designed to handle low-confidence detections and long-term occlusions—scenarios that typically demand excessive computational overhead. Experimental evaluations on the MOT15, MOT16, MOT17, and MOT20 benchmarks demonstrate that TransMOT achieves state-of-the-art performance across all datasets.
humanized_abstract: Object tracking in video sequences is a key step for robust multi-object tracking (MOT) which, by nature, requires modeling the spatial-temporal interactions between objects. In this work, we design a new tracking framework, named TransMOT, to model the interactions between objects by using graph transformers, and model the object trajectories as a sequence of sparse weighted graphs to handle complex dense scenes. It only needs a spatial graph transformer encoder and a temporal transformer encoder, a spatial graph transformer decoder to deal with the graphs. Compared with the original Transformer model, the proposed model is more efficient and achieves a better tracking performance. To further improve the efficiency and robustness, we propose a cascade association strategy for handling the low-confidence detections and long-term occlusions, which usually require more computations. Experiments are carried out on MOT15, MOT16, MOT17 and MOT20 benchmarks and results show that the proposed TransMOT achieves the state-of-the-art performance on all the four datasets.

Compared with the original Transformer model, the proposed model is more efficient and achieves a better tracking performance. To further improve the efficiency and robustness, we propose a cascade association strategy for handling the low-confidence detections and long-term occlusions, which usually require more computations. Experiments are carried out on MOT15, MOT16, MOT17 and MOT20 benchmarks and results show that the proposed TransMOT achieves the state-of-the-art performance on all the four datasets.

Experimental evaluations on the MOT15, MOT16, MOT17, and MOT20 benchmarks demonstrate that TransMOT achieves state-of-the-art performance across all datasets.
